eli3: Hybrid search combines two complementary retrieval approaches: sparse retrieval (BM25 — keyword matching) and dense retrieval (bi-encoder — semantic similarity). Sparse retrieval excels at exact keyword matches and rare terms. Dense retrieval excels at semantic similarity and paraphrases. Combining them captures both signals: exact match AND semantic relevance.

Why hybrid? BM25 misses: 'automobile accident' query won't find 'car crash' document. Dense misses: 'API error code 429' query may return semantically similar text rather than the exact error code. Hybrid search hedges both failure modes. Combination: merge and re-rank the two result lists (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) or weighted score combination. Hybrid search consistently outperforms either alone on diverse query types.

Hybrid search is the current production standard for enterprise search and RAG. Elasticsearch and OpenSearch support hybrid search natively (BM25 + kNN). Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant support sparse-dense hybrid vectors. The optimal α (weight between sparse and dense scores) depends on the query distribution and must be tuned empirically — but α=0.5 (equal weight) is a strong default.

whenWhy.title: Production RAG with diverse query types
whenWhy.body: Real-world queries span the spectrum from exact keyword lookups ('What is the refund policy paragraph 3.2?') to conceptual questions ('How does the company handle returns?'). Sparse search handles the first; dense search handles the second. A hybrid system handles both without needing to pre-classify the query type. For enterprise RAG over internal documents (legal, HR, technical manuals), hybrid search improves recall by 10–20% over dense-only search across diverse query types.

whenWhy.title: Rare terms and proper nouns
whenWhy.body: Embedding models often struggle with rare technical terms, product names, error codes, and proper nouns that weren't frequent in training data. 'OOM error in CUDA kernel allocator' — an embedding model may not capture this precisely. BM25 matches on exact tokens: if the document contains 'OOM error', 'CUDA', 'kernel', 'allocator', it gets a high BM25 score. Hybrid search includes BM25 to ensure such exact-match queries find the right document even when semantic embeddings fail.

whenWhy.title: Reducing sensitivity to embedding model choice
whenWhy.body: Dense search quality varies significantly across domains and embedding models. A general-purpose embedding model (text-embedding-3-small) may be suboptimal for medical or legal text. BM25 is domain-agnostic — it only relies on term frequency statistics in your corpus. Hybrid search is more robust to embedding model quality: even if the dense component is weaker, the BM25 component provides a reliable floor. This makes hybrid search a lower-risk default when you cannot fine-tune an embedding model.

deepDiveIntro: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) merges ranked result lists from BM25 and dense retrieval without needing to normalize their very different score scales. Each document's fused score is the sum of 1 divided by (60 plus its rank) across all systems. Documents that rank highly in both lists accumulate more score, naturally surfacing multi-system agreement. The constant 60 prevents a rank-1 document from completely dominating. [1]

A convex combination instead assigns a weight to each system's raw score and adds them — for example, 50% dense plus 50% sparse. This requires normalizing both score types to the same range first, which is tricky in practice. The mixing weight can be tuned to favor whichever system performs better on your query distribution. RRF is usually preferred for its simplicity; score combination is used when precise tuning via A/B testing is feasible. [1]

deepDive.heading: Sparse Retrieval: BM25
deepDive.body: BM25 scores a document by summing, over all query terms, the product of the term's rarity across the corpus and a saturating count of how often it appears in the document, adjusted for document length. Default parameters work well out of the box. BM25 uses an inverted index so retrieval is fast without a GPU. Its weakness is vocabulary mismatch: synonyms of a query term score zero even if the meaning is the same. [1]

deepDive.heading: Dense Retrieval: Bi-encoder ANN Search
deepDive.body: Dense retrieval encodes both the query and all documents into fixed-size vectors, then finds the closest document vectors to the query vector using approximate nearest neighbor search. HNSW graphs support sub-linear query time with very high recall, making it practical at hundreds of millions of documents. Larger corpora can use compressed indexes that trade a small amount of recall for a large reduction in memory. [2]

deepDive.heading: Sparse-Dense Fusion with RRF
deepDive.body: To fuse results, retrieve the top 100 documents from BM25 and the top 100 from dense retrieval separately. For each document, add up the reciprocal of its rank in each list, using 60 as a smoothing constant. Documents that appear near the top in both lists receive the highest combined scores. Documents absent from a list receive a rank of 101, contributing only a small amount. No score normalization is needed. [1]

deepDive.heading: Learned Sparse Retrieval: SPLADE
deepDive.body: SPLADE learns sparse document representations that can be indexed in a standard inverted index, just like BM25, but with vocabulary expanded to include semantically related terms rather than just exact matches. A BERT encoder produces a vector over the full vocabulary, and a sparsity-inducing activation keeps most entries at zero. This gives BM25-speed retrieval with near-dense-retrieval quality, and it outperforms both on diverse benchmark collections. [2]

takeaways: Hybrid search: BM25 (exact match) + dense (semantic) — handles diverse query types better than either alone. [1]
RRF: rank-based fusion, no score normalization needed, k=60 default — simple and effective. [1]
HNSW: O(log n) ANN query, ~98% recall@10 — standard for dense retrieval vector index. [2]
SPLADE: learned sparse vectors for inverted index — semantic + exact match without two separate systems. [2]
Optimal α for convex combination is query-distribution-dependent — tune empirically on validation set. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you normalize BM25 and dense scores before combining them?
edgeCases.a: BM25 scores: unbounded positive floats. Cosine similarity: [-1, 1] or [0, 1] with normalized embeddings. Direct addition is meaningless without normalization. Methods: (1) Min-max normalization: score_norm = (score - min) / (max - min) — maps each system's scores to [0,1]; (2) Z-score normalization: (score - mean) / std; (3) Sigmoid: maps to (0,1). Problem: normalization is query-dependent (min/max change per query), and outliers affect normalization. This is why RRF is preferred over score combination in practice — it's rank-based and doesn't require score normalization. For score combination with a tunable α, collect min/max statistics over a query distribution and normalize accordingly. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between hybrid search and query expansion?
edgeCases.a: Hybrid search: run sparse AND dense retrieval in parallel, merge results — combines two retrieval modalities simultaneously. Query expansion: augment the original query with synonyms or related terms, then run a single retrieval system (usually BM25) with the expanded query. Example: 'dog' → expand to 'dog OR puppy OR canine' for BM25 retrieval. Query expansion improves sparse retrieval's recall for synonyms without needing a second retrieval system. Hybrid search is generally more effective than query expansion alone because it captures both exact-match and semantic similarity in a principled way. They can also be combined: query expansion + hybrid search. [1]

sources.label: Robertson & Zaragoza (2009) — The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond, Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval
Formal et al. (2021) — SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model for First Stage Ranking, SIGIR 2021
